body.notes: Participation in IETF meetings constitutes agreement to the Note Well, which permits recording, transcription, and public archival of proceedings. See also BCP 78 (RFC 5378) and BCP 79 (RFC 8179) for IPR policies.

body.title: Guidelines for QUIC Multipath Extension over SCION

transcript.text: There was another team. Okay. Hello. off. Oh, that's Check. Check. Do we have It sounds like It sounds even like there's a little bit of feedback, which is neat. Um, confirm. [Music] So, welcome to the Pathawware Networking Research Group. Uh, this is IETF123. This is July 2025. We are in Madrid. If if these are not correct, then you are in the wrong time and space. uh and should um you know find the time and space you're looking for. Um welcome to Panergy. Uh we have a relatively quick agenda today, but I'm going to go ahead and start with um the note. Well, uh this is actually we usually aren't on Monday, so this might be the first time some of the people in this room have seen this. Um so the RTF follows the Interpol property rights disclo disclosure rules for the IETF. Um if you are everything that you um say uh during this meeting or on the mailing list is a contribution to uh the IRTF uh you should be aware of um all of the rules on these slides. Um you uh actually we should we should change these slides for the next ones. Uh you should also be aware that there is a code of conduct for uh the ITF and any harassment procedures for the ITF. In addition, there is now uh a separate code of conduct for the IRTF. um you can use the search engine of your choice to uh find that and make yourself familiar with it. Um so if you are in person, please scan the QR code in the microphone or go to meet echo via a mobile device to the um uh the on-site tool. Uh this is uh necessary for joining the meet echo queue. It also allows us to count how many people are in the meeting uh for the blue sheets which are neither sheets nor blue anymore. Um if you are on mute echo please keep audio and video off unless you're speaking uh to speak you can join the queue and we will uh enable you. Um for speakers actually we're going to manage both of these for you so you can uh avoid the or you can ignore uh those uh uh that directive uh wanted uh minutes taker. Hi Dirk. How's it going? It's good to see you. Huh? Yeah, please do. Yep. Good. So, uh quick agenda today. Uh you are here uh introduction agenda notewell. Uh tman seeska will be talking about um network awareness acknowledgements in quick uh followed by a quick update on the state of the scion drafts through the ISS uh and uh then we will have balance of time for an open mic. So with that, come on. Come up. >> There you go. >> Yeah, that one. >> Good. Uh good morning. Um I might want to adjust. Yeah. >> Good morning. My name is Tman Seshka. I'm from ETH Zurich. Uh and I'm here I would like to propose a new internet draft with guidelines for running a quick multipath extension over sign. Um so these are guidelines for implementations of libraries. So not for end users or uh applications or other parties. So I'm just going to give a brief overview and then talk about benefits and challenges of running quick multipath over can. So quick multipath the multipath extension for quick I think it's currently iteration 15 and as far as I can tell it's pretty close to being finalized. Um what we're interested here in this context is the explicit path management of multiple paths. So the ability to create paths and remove paths um explicitly also the path ID which goes beyond the foruple which in turn allows us to have multiple paths per four tpple um for scan is a pathware networking technology for interd domain routing there are currently two drafts one for the control plane and one for the data plane and there are two things we are mainly interested in in this context that's assigned network address It consists of ISDS number um IP address and port. So and the ISDS number is basically the AS number uh where the end host is located and ISD number is like a grouping number for ASS. So and the second thing we are interested in is scan path. So paths are like a series of as of interfaces and links with metadata. So if you look at the example, it's like on the left we have a client that is located in AS number three. Um and then there's an AS in the middle and then on the right side we have a server located in AS2 and for all the links and AS we get MTU information, latency information, bandwidth information, uh and the hop count internal to the AS's. Um the lifetime of a path is usually hours or days and then the path expires so it's not valid anymore. And also important in this context is that we often may have hundreds of paths between two endpoints. So could we just run quick multipath over sign? And the answer is yes. We don't actually need to do anything. We could just run it but we would miss out on some benefits and we would also run into some security problems. So technically it works but you should only do that in a safe environment. So first look at the benefits. Um we have obviously path metadata and the ability to explicitly select paths. Um for example that allows us to select two paths that are completely disjointed. Um one would be the main path and the other one would be backup path. And then if the main path fails for some reason um the likelihood that the backup path is also affected is very low. Um, another thing we could do is can do is use the benefits and latency metadata that comes with the uh path for initial selection because remember we have a lot of path potentially. Another thing we can do is avoid untrusted ass. Untrusted also just means in this context having a history of poor quality of service for example. uh specifically for congestion control. Uh what we can do is we can look at the path and detect where these paths overlap and then either avoid overlapping paths or if we don't cannot avoid it, we can look at the segments or the links where they overlap and choose it in a way where we know that the link is unlikely to be a bottleneck because for example it has a very high bandwidth or something. Um that in turn allows us to simplify congestion control. So we can run congestion control separately on each path and we don't need to really look up how it works on the other path. Um for RT estimation we can use a latency metadata for initial path selection again because we may have a lot of paths. Um and then we can basically if we don't have a a latency critical application we can just skip RT estimation um and use like the metadata. The thing is the metadata may be a bit outdated. The meta data that comes latency metadata that comes with a pass may be hours or days old. But I think usually you can argue um that it's not going to be orders of magnitude different from what you have like a few hours later. Um, another thing we can do with RT estimation is because we have really explicit path selection and pass knowledge, we can ensure that the forward and backward probe uh exactly use exactly same path. So that's also beneficial for RT estimation. Um, oh sorry and then we have MTU discovery and again it's a bit similar. we get the MTU data and we can pre-seelelect paths and rule out paths that have a too low MTU. Um and then we could still run MTU discovery obviously if you want. So in summary for the benefits um the guidelines is trivially just look at the topology and metadata to simplify um probing and other algorithms. Um then second do not probe all paths because that could be too many and that would be almost a denial of service attack. Um if you do that um then on the server side at least we should can simplify RT. Um yeah by all answering on the same path we should also answer on the same path because we want to respect uh the client's choice. The client may have chosen a path for a reason including security reasons. So I think it's a good idea to um respect that if possible. Uh and related in the quick RFC it already says like the client is responsible for initiating migrations which can be interpreted as basically new path. Okay let's look at the challenges. Um that's mainly three related to network address to path identity and path life cycle. So for the network address on the top you see like two sign network addresses in the front the ISDS number and then IP and port and what you can see here is that they only differ in the last digit of the AS numbers. So the IP address and the port is the same for two different end hosts. So the two tpple of uh port and endo uh IP and port is not enough to uniquely identify an end host. And that's uh of course critical for path validation because usually if um the endto changes we want to trigger path validation and that means we need to actually look at the ISDS number. So we have a three tle that we have to look at. Uh there are different ways to solve that. The cleanest way is obviously to um adjust all implementations of quick multipath to actually look at the three tuple but that's yeah obviously a lot of work. So what we also have seen in scan libraries is that they do IP port mangling. So they mangle the AS code into the IP and port which ensures that if the AS number changes also the port and IP changes which in turn triggers path validation. Um but yeah obviously that can have unintended implications uh on application level. For example, if the application expects there to be a certain IP address abroad um path ID is similar um just to give a big back bit background sign libraries may just try to look at like a normal UDP socket. So what they do if you just give them an IP address they will internally look up a path select a pass and then use that path and um the pass may then uh yeah if the path has a problem like it's it breaks and reports a problem or it expires the library will just update the path and what should happen here if the path changes we should reset the congestion control and RTT algorithms. Um but here we can see that just observing even the network address including ISDS is not sufficient because the path may just change somewhere in between uh and the end points. So the guideline here is that we should ensure that the path changes still reset congestion control and IT estimation. And one way to kind of ensure that is that the sign layer should disable this automatic path updates if the path breaks or expires. Um at least when it's using quick multipath because in other scenarios this automatic update can actually be quite useful. And then if the path breaks we let the quick layer or the application layer um find a new path and finally the path life cycle. So sign libraries may maintain a mapping from path ID to network address or from the mangled IP port to an unmangled address. And the problem here is really how to maintain the life cycle. Um it's easy to detect a new path for a library but it's um difficult to detect a path abandoned a path closed or just a path a packet that is rejected becomes from because it comes from a attacker or something and is invalid. So basically the guideline here is to on the server side at least try to not maintain any state and uh instead let the quick multipath layer application layer uh manage path. Um unfortunately that's not always possible. So for example you can imagine a proxy that on one end understands can and on the other side of the proxy there's just some servers that are connected via normal IP. Um in that case the server has to maintain state. So yeah we yeah we have to see how that can be solved. Um so in summary um when the network address changes we need to trigger path validation just to make sure this is quick path validation not uh puny or path validation which is a different thing. Um when the path changes we should trigger CC or RTD estimation reset. Um I don't think we must do it here because even in current internet sometimes the path may change and the end hosts may never learn of it. So the I think the algorithms are quite resilient actually to having a path change. Um then for the server we should try to avoid state. Um it's also the security implications because it kind of um exposes you to memory exhaustion attacks or similar things. Uh and finally the sign layer should not update broken or explicit paths. Um yeah and that's already uh most of the things that are in the draft. So now I have some question for example we were thinking um is it a good idea to make this draft only about scan or should be extended to pants in general that may be more interesting because I think there are many common points with other networking technologies. uh there also um security considerations and I think here the problem is more that they can be very specific. So that's more an argument for having it only about scan because otherwise you will have tons of security considerations for different technologies and if you actually make it about all or many pants then of how many PS do we need to cover in order to be complete? Um yeah that's uh one question I don't really know the answer to. Um yeah, also I'm happy to get any general feedback suggest uh suggestions or anything else and if anyone would be willing to review the document would be also very much appreciated. Thank you. >> Questions from the floor? >> Hello. >> Oh wait, uh Lucas is already in queue. So go ahead uh please go ahead and put yourself in the queue and meet mute echo. You can put yourself in the queue and meet mute echo. Uh Lucas, go ahead. >> Hi Lucas Pard, Cloudflare and co-chair of the quickworking group. I'm aware of Zion. I'm by no means an expert at all in it. Um and I've only been at Pan a few times over the last few years. Sorry CHZ. Um but yeah, this this is this is interesting work. As you say, the multipath quick extension is is has already been through one round of working group last call. The design is basically locked in. We're just eyeing out some of the final few issues. Um I'm no expert in that either. I know enough to be um you know we sorry uh own an implementation of quick and we've had to deal with um some of the path awareness of the the quick library itself and how the applications integrate with that even though we're not doing the full multiath extension. So this is kind of where my question is leading of quick is kind of weird that it does have a notion of multiath just in RSC 9000 right we have connection migration just so happens that those paths are not going to be used in parallel but that you would transfer from one to the other one of the examples is passive migration where we have things like nats Brian can probably talk at great length about those um but in summary you know something happens within the network that changes the client's tupil which triggers a migration event in the server to be able to um assign that new packet from a different tupil to the correct quick connection and ensure that the the migration events that you mentioned would happen. So this got me thinking and I haven't been able to read the draft or all of this stuff. How much of the the guidance or the challenges that you're talking about just apply too quick if it was to be operating over some other form of network that has different path awareness challenges? That's a maybe too vague a question, but um maybe maybe we can abstract some of the stuff you have here or break it out to be kind of what you're suggesting. Maybe this is considerations for quick and path aware networks that we didn't think about five years ago when we were targeting IP. Um I'd be happy to to to speak more about that off list. Um and I also the other question was have you spoken to to the multipath quick authors about this and shared your ideas because they might they're way more familiar with it than I am. They might have some good feedback for you too. >> Okay. Um yeah, thanks. Um, I haven't really spoken to anyone yet, so it's my um that's a pretty new work. Um, I do think a lot of that also applies to quick, but I started looking at multipath quick, so I haven't really tried to figure out what is actually not specific to multipath, but I guess quite a number of things. >> Yeah, I I I' I'd recommend maybe posting an email to the quick working group list. I'm looking around. I'm not seeing many folks from that um thing just because I think this is a really interesting uh piece of work. So yeah. >> Okay. I I would note I don't think I see any of the multipath quick authors in this room but I have seen many of them at this meeting. Um so it might also be useful just to send to like the draft. Um you know there's like draft there's a there's an email expansion for like the draft authors of a draft and say hi I've got this thing. Would you have any time to meet on the side? that that actually would probably be a very good use of everyone's time. >> Yeah. >> Cool. Thank you very much. >> Good. Thanks. >> Did you figure it out? >> Okay. So, so if if I may, I've taken a quick look at this. Um it seems like there's one sort of like large area which is like hey if you're dealing with a different network architecture with quick you need to have some concrete mapping of that that architectures addressing scheme into how quick does pass right like so if I were thinking of like a multiath quick implementation it would have like you know a quick connection um uh sort of object and then it would have underneath that quick path objects and you'd need to basically say you know this is the way to map >> you my addressing scheme for the path, right? Because that's a very important point with with Scion is like in a lot of cases your Scion your IP address part of your Scion packet is probably going to be privately routed within that as right like so realistically I don't know how that's implemented in Scan lab but like those are not always publicly routable IP addresses inside the scion side of the network. Yeah. >> Um, and then there's also the idea of like, hey, are these two IP addresses like when they're not deployed like that, it's like are these two IP addresses the same endpoint over different path? Like there's there's nuance there to work out. And I think that like working out that nuance within this within the scope of the scion multiath work makes it possible to do you know the idea of what is a path inside quick multiath a lot more um rigorously right than if you're just looking at the single network architecture. >> Okay. And then maybe this idea then to potentially propose an extension to the quick multipath >> um kind of specifies that. Yeah, >> it's cool. >> Sounds good. >> Uh, as to you know, putting putting my my RFC uh 927 author hat on. Uh, to your third question, I would say just doing it for scion is great, right? Like so for for the number of network architectures you consider, there's basically only three numbers one, two, and or zero, one, and greater than one, right? Like so just the scion considerations I think are a very valuable contribution. >> Okay. Uh yeah, thanks >> Other points questions. It's Yeah. Okay, come on up. Uh but do identify yourself so we for minutes. >> Okay. Uh Mway from Siha University. Uh so my question is about uh yeah you said uh when using uh quick multipass uh does sound layer should disable automatic path changes? Yeah. So my question is uh will it decrease the performance or security of sc >> the performance of security of >> of of scion decrease the performance of the sec security if uh if s layer disabled automatic path change >> um I don't think it affects so if I understand the correction the question correctly is whether um disabling the automatic path change um affects security or security performance. Um I don't think it does. It's just um if a path change I mean the the path change is that should only be disabled if the path change ability is still in the application or in the quick layer. So someone will actually notice that the path is broken or error will be triggered and a new path will then be created on a different um level of the stack. Um so I don't think uh that's a security concern. Does that answer the question? >> Okay. Okay. Okay. Thank you. >> Okay. Thanks. Going once, going twice. Thank you very much. Uh, and do definitely find the quick multipath folks all the way. I think that's >> Yeah. >> How long are you here? >> Um, Thursday evening. >> Okay, cool. Yeah, you should be able to find some time. >> Thank you. PowerPoint. >> All right. Um, so now I uh this is Nicolola from scan association. I would like to give a very quick update regarding the scion course spec. So uh this is uh these are documents that we discussed here quite a while ago and then they have they are going through the ice uh review process. So this happens a bit outside of um you know the normal uh discussion but uh as there still discussions here I'd like to try to feedback what is happening. So the core specification of scion is in these uh three drafts that you see on top. So these are the core components and yeah then there are this additional topics as the one that tilman presented. So uh what happened since last ITF we actually got a really great um control plane uh review uh from Albaro and uh we have been doing quite some work on the drafts. We still have some left. Um one of it concerns how these scan numbers are represented uh making it consistent with the drafts and also updating uh uh the reference to the scan number registry. So this is a function that is currently carried on by uh one of the vendors anapaya and we are assign association taking this over. We also mandated uh the use of a certain uh number for unspecified interface ID which is something used in the control plane uh and we need this for interpity between vendors. We have done also some general housekeeping in the drafts including figures, needs and uh normative language and so on. We are actually not done yet. So parts of the updates are already in the latest uh draft versions that you see down here. But there are other uh topics where we are still uh thinking a little a little bit about the feedback received. One of it is the use of gRPC. So if you look at the draft for example there is a lot of uh protobuff uh definitions uh because the control plane in scan uses gRPC and we are thinking how do we uh uh list them properly in the draft there's not so many documents that uh that use protobuff uh we are also thinking about the security considerations of using gRPC on top of uh big http3 uh we will also be adding these metadata extensions these are not part of the course spec today but uh we have seen that they are currently supported by uh by all the implementations they exist. So after discussion with the reviewers we uh we feel that uh these are worth to be included. This is also what the quick draft is using for for example getting latency and uh bandwidth and so on out of the scion pads. We will be adding some clarifications on MTUs um and how this MTU can be taken out of these scion pads. I think uh that's that's it for now. We are still looking for reviewers. So if you are pass our networking enthusiasts and are having a read at at our drafts uh we will be very happy if you drop us an email with some feedback or uh if you want to do this within the ice process if you drop an email to uh the ice editor Elliot. Uh that said, uh thank you. And if there's any questions about the spec, uh I'm around or I'm here. Any questions, comments, remarks, claims, complaints about the cookies? That's Wednesday. Um All right, thank you very much. Uh, with that we will go to um something we've never had time for uh PANRG open mic. Um, if anyone has any uh questions, things to introduce etc etc etc for the path networking research group please come up. Seven seconds of uncomfortable silence complete. Thank you all very much. Enjoy the rest of your week and we will see you in Montreal. Yes.
